answer:
Across the four texts, the trajectory of severity for adultery (paradarika / saṃgrahaṇa) moves from broad and harshly caste-stratified (Manu), through a simplified three-tier scale with prayaschitta framing (Visnu), to a more streamlined legal grid (Yajnavalkya), and finally to the most procedurally detailed and exception-rich treatment (Narada).

**Manu** (Manusmrti ch. 8, verses 8.352–8.385): The most elaborate graduated-by-caste scheme. Adultery is defined broadly — secret conversation, touching ornaments, sitting together on a bed (Mn_8.356–8.358). A non-Brahmana receives death for adultery with any of the four varnas (Mn_8.359). The caste matrix is fine-grained: a Shudra who has intercourse with a twice-born guarded woman loses his limb and all property (Mn_8.374); a Vaishya loses all property after a year's imprisonment; a Kshatriya is fined 1,000 and head-shaved with urine (Mn_8.375). A Brahmana with a guarded Brahmana woman is fined 1,000 (Mn_8.378) — the 'capital' punishment for a Brahmana is head-shaving (Mn_8.379), not death. The adulterous woman is devoured by dogs (Mn_8.371); the adulterous man is burned on a heated iron bed (Mn_8.372). Severity correlates strictly with caste inversion: anuloma (higher man, lower woman) is fined; pratiloma (lower man, higher woman) is lethal.

**Visnu** (Visnu Smriti ch. 5, verses Vi_5.40–5.43; ch. 37, Vi_37.9; ch. 53, Vi_53.1–2): A simplified three-tier scale without the elaborate Manu matrix. Same-varna adultery = highest amercement (Vi_5.40); intercourse with a lower-varna woman = middle amercement (Vi_5.41); intercourse with an outcaste woman = death (Vi_5.43). Adultery with another's wife is also listed as an upapātaka (minor sin) in ch. 37 (Vi_37.9), attracting a cāndrāyaṇa or parāka penance. The prayaschitta for illicit intercourse / intercourse with another's wife is one year's bark-garment vow with prājāpatya penance (Vi_53.1–2). Notably, the adulterer who violates another's wife is categorized as an ātatāyin — a felonious aggressor who may lawfully be killed (Vi_5.193). Overall less granular than Manu on the criminal side but consistent in death for the most aggravated case.

**Yajnavalkya** (Yajnavalkya Smriti ch. 2, verses Yj_2.283–2.294): Streamlined and procedurally oriented. Arrest is by objective signs — seizing hair, signs of lust, confession (Yj_2.283). Touching the garment-knot, breasts, thighs, or hair, or conversing at an improper place, constitutes adultery (Yj_2.284). If the woman forbids it: she pays 100, the man 200; if both forbid it, same punishment as for actual intercourse (Yj_2.285). The three-tier caste scale: same-caste intercourse = highest fine; anuloma (direct order) = middle fine; pratiloma (reverse order) = death for the man + cutting off of the woman's ears (Yj_2.286). For abducting a maiden in reverse order, death is also prescribed (Yj_2.287). More concise than Manu, somewhat harsher on the woman than Narada.

**Narada** (Naradasmrti section 12, verses Nar. 12.60–12.78; Nar. 14.5): The most differentiated and exception-rich treatment. The same caste structure obtains: transgression with a higher-class woman = highest amercement; lower-class woman = middle amercement; pratiloma union = death (Nar. 12.69). Violation of an unwilling maiden = cutting off of two fingers; of a higher-class woman = death with confiscation (Nar. 12.70). Crucially, Narada introduces a carve-out absent from the others: if a husband is depraved, abandoned, impotent, or consumptive, another man who approaches his wife with her consent commits no offense of violence (Nar. 12.61) — a notable relaxation. Violating any of a long list of 'teacher's bed' relations (mother, sister, teacher's wife, queen, etc.) = cutting off of the penis (Nar. 12.74). Narada also classifies the violation of another's wife as the highest grade of sāhasa (crime of violence, Nar. 14.5), placing it alongside murder by poison or weapon.

**Trajectory summary**: Manu is the harshest and most granular — it lists the most varna-specific punishments and prescribes spectacular corporal/capital punishments (burning, devoured by dogs) even for intra-varna adultery when the woman is 'guarded.' Visnu simplifies the criminal scheme to three tiers but adds the ātatāyin categorization (the adulterer may be killed outright) and handles expiation through prayaschitta. Yajnavalkya systematizes the law procedurally (evidentiary criteria, graduated fines) and is moderate in tone. Narada is the most procedurally sophisticated and introduces the most exceptions and mitigating contexts, while also escalating certain categories (teacher's bed violations = castration; adultery = highest sāhasa).
